Optimal Transport for Machine Learners

Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceStatistical Optimal TransportGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Statistical Optimal Transport

Optimal transport is rarely evaluated on population measures directly. In machine learning and statistics, the inputs are usually empirical laws, histograms, simulated particles or minibatches, and the central question is therefore no longer only how to compute OT, but how OT behaves as a random estimator. This chapter studies this statistical layer: qualitative consistency of empirical measures, non-asymptotic sample-complexity rates, and asymptotic bias--variance decompositions for exact and regularized transport costs.

This statistical convergence is conceptually different from the algorithmic convergence studied in the previous chapter. There the marginals and the temperature were fixed and one asked how Sinkhorn iterates approach a regularized optimizer. Here the number of samples grows, the empirical measures themselves move, and the regularization parameter may either remain fixed or be sent to zero. The resulting picture explains why exact OT is statistically expensive in high intrinsic dimension, why fixed-temperature Sinkhorn has smoother parametric fluctuations, and why approximating exact OT with entropy always involves a bias--variance tradeoff.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Law of Large Numbers and Central Limit Theorem¶

Before discussing sample complexity, it is useful to separate consistency from rates. If X1,…,XnX_1,\ldots,X_nX1​,…,Xn​ are i.i.d. samples with common law α\alphaα, the associated empirical measure is the random probability measure

α^n:=1n∑i=1nδXi.\hat\alpha_n \eqdef \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\delta_{X_i}.α^n​:=n1​i=1∑n​δXi​​.
(1)

The ordinary law of large numbers says that empirical averages converge to expectations. In measure language this means that α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ converges weakly toward α\alphaα, because testing α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ against a bounded continuous function φ\varphiφ gives the sample average n−1∑iφ(Xi)n^{-1}\sum_i\varphi(X_i)n−1∑i​φ(Xi​). Wasserstein distances strengthen this statement by also recording moment convergence. Thus, if α\alphaα has a finite ppp-th moment, the empirical law converges to α\alphaα in Wp\Wass_pWp​, almost surely and in ppp-th mean in the sense that EWp(α^n,α)p→0\mathbb E\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^p\to0EWp​(α^n​,α)p→0. This is the qualitative consistency statement behind empirical OT plug-in estimators: empirical transport distances converge to their population counterparts once the sampled laws themselves converge in Wasserstein distance. It says nothing yet about the speed, which is the topic of Sample Complexity.

Proposition: Empirical Law of Large Numbers in Wp\Wass_pWp​

Let (X,d)(\Xx,d)(X,d) be a Polish metric space, let p≥1p\geq1p≥1, and let α∈Pp(X)\alpha\in\Pp_p(\Xx)α∈Pp​(X). Let (Xi)i≥1(X_i)_{i\geq1}(Xi​)i≥1​ be i.i.d. random variables with law α\alphaα, and define α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ by (1). Then

α^n⇀α,Wp(α^n,α)⟶0\hat\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alpha, \qquad \Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)\longrightarrow0α^n​⇀α,Wp​(α^n​,α)⟶0
(2)

almost surely. Moreover,

E Wp(α^n,α)p⟶0.\mathbb E\,\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^p\longrightarrow0.EWp​(α^n​,α)p⟶0.
(3)
Proof

Fix a reference point x0∈Xx_0\in\Xxx0​∈X, and write r(x)=d(x,x0)r(x)=d(x,x_0)r(x)=d(x,x0​). Since X\XxX is Polish, the weak topology on P(X)\Pp(\Xx)P(X) admits a countable convergence-determining class (φk)k≥1⊂Cb(X)(\varphi_k)_{k\geq1}\subset C_b(\Xx)(φk​)k≥1​⊂Cb​(X). For each fixed kkk, the strong law of large numbers gives

∫φk  dα^n=1n∑i=1nφk(Xi)⟶Eφk(X1)=∫φk  dα\int \varphi_k\,\d\hat\alpha_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\varphi_k(X_i) \longrightarrow \mathbb E\varphi_k(X_1) = \int\varphi_k\,\d\alpha∫φk​dα^n​=n1​i=1∑n​φk​(Xi​)⟶Eφk​(X1​)=∫φk​dα
(4)

almost surely. Intersecting these probability-one events over the countable set of indices gives convergence against every φk\varphi_kφk​, hence weak convergence α^n⇀α\hat\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaα^n​⇀α.

The moment condition α∈Pp(X)\alpha\in\Pp_p(\Xx)α∈Pp​(X) means ∫rp  dα<+∞\int r^p\,\d\alpha<+\infty∫rpdα<+∞. Applying the strong law again to r(X1)pr(X_1)^pr(X1​)p gives

∫rp  dα^n=1n∑i=1nr(Xi)p⟶∫rp  dα\int r^p\,\d\hat\alpha_n = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n r(X_i)^p \longrightarrow \int r^p\,\d\alpha∫rpdα^n​=n1​i=1∑n​r(Xi​)p⟶∫rpdα
(5)

almost surely. Weak convergence plus convergence of ppp-th moments is equivalent to Wp\Wass_pWp​ convergence on Pp(X)\Pp_p(\Xx)Pp​(X), so Wp(α^n,α)→0\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)\to0Wp​(α^n​,α)→0 almost surely.

For convergence in expectation, set An=∫rp  dα^nA_n=\int r^p\,\d\hat\alpha_nAn​=∫rpdα^n​ and M=∫rp  dαM=\int r^p\,\d\alphaM=∫rpdα. The triangle inequality through the Dirac mass δx0\delta_{x_0}δx0​​, followed by (a+b)p≤2p−1(ap+bp)(a+b)^p\leq2^{p-1}(a^p+b^p)(a+b)p≤2p−1(ap+bp), gives

Wp(α^n,α)p≤2p−1(An+M).\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^p \leq 2^{p-1}(A_n+M).Wp​(α^n​,α)p≤2p−1(An​+M).
(6)

The family (An)n(A_n)_n(An​)n​ is uniformly integrable. By the de la Vallee--Poussin criterion, choose a convex superlinear function Ψ\PsiΨ such that EΨ(r(X1)p)<+∞\mathbb E\Psi(r(X_1)^p)<+\inftyEΨ(r(X1​)p)<+∞; Jensen’s inequality gives

EΨ(An)≤1n∑i=1nEΨ(r(Xi)p)=EΨ(r(X1)p).\mathbb E\Psi(A_n) \leq \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\mathbb E\Psi(r(X_i)^p) = \mathbb E\Psi(r(X_1)^p).EΨ(An​)≤n1​i=1∑n​EΨ(r(Xi​)p)=EΨ(r(X1​)p).
(7)

Thus (An+M)n(A_n+M)_n(An​+M)n​, and hence (Wp(α^n,α)p)n(\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^p)_n(Wp​(α^n​,α)p)n​, is uniformly integrable. Together with almost-sure convergence to zero, this implies EWp(α^n,α)p→0\mathbb E\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^p\to0EWp​(α^n​,α)p→0.

Central-Limit Fluctuations¶

The previous proposition is a law-of-large-numbers statement: a random empirical measure converges to the law that generated it. The central limit theorem describes a different, fluctuation-scale limit. As recalled in Remark Remark: Central limit theorem, if (Xi)i≥1(X_i)_{i\geq1}(Xi​)i≥1​ are centered i.i.d. random vectors with identity covariance, the law of n−1/2∑iXin^{-1/2}\sum_i X_in−1/2∑i​Xi​ converges weakly toward a Gaussian. Equivalently, if α\alphaα is the common law of the XiX_iXi​, this law is the rescaled convolution (D1/n)♯α∗n(D_{1/\sqrt n})_\sharp\alpha^{*n}(D1/n​​)♯​α∗n. Wasserstein distances make this qualitative convergence quantitative. The next result is a W1\Wass_1W1​ form of the Berry--Esseen theorem: it controls the error uniformly over all 1-Lipschitz test functions.

show_book_figure("matching-quantitative-clt")

Central-limit theorem for normalized Bernoulli sums. Starting from α0=12(δ−1+δ1)\alpha_0=\frac12(\delta_{-1}+\delta_1)α0​=21​(δ−1​+δ1​), the law of Zn=n−1/2∑iXiZ_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_i X_iZn​=n−1/2∑i​Xi​ remains discrete, but its rescaled atom heights approach the standard Gaussian density shown in gray. The Wasserstein Berry--Esseen bound below quantifies this weak convergence by a W1\Wass_1W1​ distance.

The interactive demo varies the number of summands and the Bernoulli skew, making the Wasserstein view of weak convergence visible even while the law remains discrete.

Interactive panel. Use the number-of-summands and Bernoulli-skew controls to watch the Wasserstein CLT scaling predicted by Lipschitz test functions.

Proposition: Berry--Esseen bound in W1\Wass_1W1​

Let (Xi)i=1n(X_i)_{i=1}^n(Xi​)i=1n​ be i.i.d. real random variables with EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0, EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1 and E∣Xi∣3<+∞\mathbb{E}|X_i|^3<+\inftyE∣Xi​∣3<+∞. If αn\alpha_nαn​ is the law of n−1/2∑iXin^{-1/2}\sum_i X_in−1/2∑i​Xi​ and γ\gammaγ is the standard Gaussian law, then

W1(αn,γ)≤C E∣X1∣3n,\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) \leq \frac{C\,\mathbb{E}|X_1|^3}{\sqrt n},W1​(αn​,γ)≤n​CE∣X1​∣3​,
(8)

where CCC is a universal constant.

Proof Sketch

By Kantorovich--Rubinstein duality,

W1(αn,γ)=sup⁡Lip⁡(h)≤1∣Eh(Sn)−Eh(G)∣,Sn=n−1/2∑iXi,G∼γ.\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) = \sup_{\Lip(h)\leq1} \left|\mathbb{E}h(S_n)-\mathbb{E}h(G)\right|, \qquad S_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_iX_i, \quad G\sim\gamma.W1​(αn​,γ)=Lip(h)≤1sup​∣Eh(Sn​)−Eh(G)∣,Sn​=n−1/2i∑​Xi​,G∼γ.
(9)

For each such hhh, solve Stein’s equation fh′(x)−xfh(x)=h(x)−Eh(G)f_h'(x)-xf_h(x)=h(x)-\mathbb{E}h(G)fh′​(x)−xfh​(x)=h(x)−Eh(G). The solution has uniform derivative bounds of first and second order depending only on the Lipschitz constant of hhh. Write Sn(i)=Sn−Xi/nS_n^{(i)}=S_n-X_i/\sqrt nSn(i)​=Sn​−Xi​/n​. Since Sn(i)S_n^{(i)}Sn(i)​ is independent of XiX_iXi​, a Taylor expansion of fh′f_h'fh′​ and fhf_hfh​ around Sn(i)S_n^{(i)}Sn(i)​ cancels the first- and second-order terms by EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0 and EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1. The remainder is bounded by CE∣Xi/n∣3C\mathbb{E}|X_i/\sqrt n|^3CE∣Xi​/n​∣3. Summing over iii gives the n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 rate Berry, 1941Esseen, 1942Chen et al., 2011Bobkov, 2018Rio, 2011.

Sample Complexity¶

This section separates two statistical regimes. Exact OT resolves geometry at all spatial scales and pays rates controlled by the metric entropy, hence by the intrinsic dimension, of the support. Fixed-temperature Sinkhorn divergences smooth the dual potentials and recover parametric fluctuations, at the price of regularization bias.

The previous section proves qualitative convergence of empirical laws. The sample-complexity question is how fast this convergence happens. The sample complexity of unregularized OT suffers from the curse of dimensionality, but the relevant dimension is geometric. If the distributions are supported on a regular lower-dimensional set, for instance a d′d'd′-dimensional submanifold of Rd\RR^dRd, the empirical rate is governed by d′d'd′ rather than by the ambient dimension ddd. In the high-dimensional regime for Wp\Wass_pWp​, namely d′>2pd'>2pd′>2p, this gives the characteristic rate n−1/d′n^{-1/d'}n−1/d′. Exact OT is therefore dimension-adaptive: it sees the intrinsic dimension of the data support through its covering numbers Dudley, 1969Weed & Bach, 2019. Entropic regularization changes a different aspect of the picture: for a fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, Sinkhorn divergences have parametric n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 statistical rates, although the constant deteriorates when ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0 Genevay et al., 2019Bigot et al., 2019. Related two-sample-testing viewpoints are developed in Ramdas et al., 2017, and the large-ϵ\epsilonϵ kernel limit connects to classical MMD tests Gretton et al., 2012.

show_book_figure("sinkhorn-bias-variance-tradeoff")

Empirical fluctuations in dimensions three and six. For each sample size nnn, two independent empirical measures are drawn from the same standard Gaussian law. Exact OT follows a slower dimension-dependent scale, while MMD and the fixed-ϵ\epsilonϵ Sinkhorn divergence behave closer to the parametric n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 guide. This is a statistical illustration, not a solver benchmark.

Proposition: Empirical OT has Intrinsic-Dimension Value Rates

Let α\alphaα and β\betaβ be probability distributions with densities bounded above and below on [0,1]d[0,1]^d[0,1]d, and let α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ and β^m\hat\beta_mβ^​m​ be independent empirical measures. For d>2pd>2pd>2p, the expected empirical error for estimating the two-sample distance obeys

E∣Wp(α^n,β^m)−Wp(α,β)∣≲n−1/d+m−1/d.\mathbb E \abs{ \Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \Wass_p(\alpha,\beta) } \lesssim n^{-1/d}+m^{-1/d}.E∣∣​Wp​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Wp​(α,β)∣∣​≲n−1/d+m−1/d.
(10)

The exponent changes in low dimension, but the important message is that exact OT deteriorates with the intrinsic dimension of the support rather than necessarily with the ambient dimension. More generally, if the measures are supported on a compact set with regular d′d'd′-dimensional volume growth, so that covering numbers at scale rrr are of order r−d′r^{-d'}r−d′, the same estimate holds with ddd replaced by d′d'd′ when d′>2pd'>2pd′>2p. This includes smooth compact d′d'd′-dimensional submanifolds with densities bounded above and below with respect to their volume measure.

Proof Sketch

By the triangle inequality,

∣Wp(α^n,β^m)−Wp(α,β)∣≤Wp(α^n,α)+Wp(β^m,β).\abs{\Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta)} \leq \Wass_p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha) + \Wass_p(\hat\beta_m,\beta).∣∣​Wp​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Wp​(α,β)∣∣​≤Wp​(α^n​,α)+Wp​(β^​m​,β).
(11)

For the one-sample term, partition [0,1]d[0,1]^d[0,1]d into dyadic cubes. At scale 2−j2^{-j}2−j, empirical mass fluctuations over the cells are of order n−1/22jd/2n^{-1/2}2^{jd/2}n−1/22jd/2, while moving this excess mass inside cells costs 2−j2^{-j}2−j. Summing the multiscale contributions up to the scale where the expected number of samples per cell is order one gives 2−J2^{-J}2−J with 2Jd≃n2^{Jd}\simeq n2Jd≃n, hence n−1/dn^{-1/d}n−1/d. On a support with d′d'd′-dimensional metric entropy, the same chaining argument uses covers with O(2jd′)O(2^{jd'})O(2jd′) cells at scale 2−j2^{-j}2−j, hence the balancing scale is 2Jd′≃n2^{Jd'}\simeq n2Jd′≃n and the rate is n−1/d′n^{-1/d'}n−1/d′. Matching lower bounds follow from packing arguments Dudley, 1969Fournier & Guillin, 2015Weed & Bach, 2019.

Proposition: MMD has a Parametric Value Rate

Let kkk be a bounded positive definite kernel with RKHS Hk\mathcal H_kHk​, and define

MMD⁡k(α,β):=∥∫k(x,⋅)  d(α−β)(x)∥Hk.\operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \norm{ \int k(x,\cdot)\,\d(\alpha-\beta)(x) }_{\mathcal H_k}.MMDk​(α,β):=∥∥​∫k(x,⋅)d(α−β)(x)∥∥​Hk​​.
(12)

If α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ and β^m\hat\beta_mβ^​m​ are independent empirical measures, then

E∣MMD⁡k(α^n,β^m)−MMD⁡k(α,β)∣≤κ(1n+1m)\mathbb E \abs{ \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) } \leq \kappa\left(\frac1{\sqrt n}+\frac1{\sqrt m}\right)E∣∣​MMDk​(α^n​,β^​m​)−MMDk​(α,β)∣∣​≤κ(n​1​+m​1​)
(13)

when k(x,x)≤κ2k(x,x)\leq\kappa^2k(x,x)≤κ2.

Proof

Let Φ(x)=k(x,⋅)\Phi(x)=k(x,\cdot)Φ(x)=k(x,⋅) be the feature map and mα=EΦ(X)m_\alpha=\mathbb E\Phi(X)mα​=EΦ(X). The reverse triangle inequality gives

∣MMD⁡k(α^n,β^m)−MMD⁡k(α,β)∣≤MMD⁡k(α^n,α)+MMD⁡k(β^m,β).\abs{ \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \operatorname{MMD}_k(\alpha,\beta) } \leq \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha) + \operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\beta_m,\beta).∣∣​MMDk​(α^n​,β^​m​)−MMDk​(α,β)∣∣​≤MMDk​(α^n​,α)+MMDk​(β^​m​,β).
(14)

Independence cancels cross terms after taking squared norms and expectation:

EMMD⁡k(α^n,α)2=1nE∥Φ(X)−mα∥Hk2=1n(Ek(X,X)−Ek(X,X′)).\mathbb E\operatorname{MMD}_k(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)^2 = \frac1n \mathbb E\norm{\Phi(X)-m_\alpha}_{\mathcal H_k}^2 = \frac1n \left( \mathbb E k(X,X)-\mathbb E k(X,X') \right).EMMDk​(α^n​,α)2=n1​E∥Φ(X)−mα​∥Hk​2​=n1​(Ek(X,X)−Ek(X,X′)).
(15)

Jensen’s inequality and k(x,x)≤κ2k(x,x)\leq\kappa^2k(x,x)≤κ2 give the displayed bound.

Proposition: Sinkhorn Divergences Interpolate the Rates

Assume α\alphaα and β\betaβ are supported in a compact subset of Rd\RR^dRd and the cost is smooth. For fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, debiased Sinkhorn divergences satisfy representative empirical bounds of the form

E∣L‾cϵ(α^n,β^m)−L‾cϵ(α,β)∣≤Cc,dϵ−d/2(1n+1m),\mathbb E \abs{ \overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m) - \overline{\mathcal L}_c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta) } \leq C_{c,d}\epsilon^{-d/2} \left( \frac1{\sqrt n}+\frac1{\sqrt m} \right),E∣∣​Lcϵ​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Lcϵ​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cc,d​ϵ−d/2(n​1​+m​1​),
(16)

up to constants and exponents depending on the precise smoothness class and support diameter. Thus regularization removes the n−1/dn^{-1/d}n−1/d curse for fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ, while the prefactor deteriorates as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0.

Proof Sketch

By the envelope theorem, the fluctuation of Lcϵ\mathcal L_c^\epsilonLcϵ​ with respect to its first marginal is controlled by the class of entropic dual potentials. The soft ccc-transform smooths these potentials at spatial scale ϵ\sqrt\epsilonϵ​ for a quadratic-type cost. Covering a bounded ddd-dimensional domain at this scale gives an effective complexity of order ϵ−d/2\epsilon^{-d/2}ϵ−d/2. Standard Rademacher or Dudley entropy bounds then give an empirical-process fluctuation of order ϵ−d/2/n\epsilon^{-d/2}/\sqrt nϵ−d/2/n​ for each marginal. Applying the same estimate to the three terms defining the debiased divergence gives the stated bound.

Remark: No free lunch when approximating exact OT

The parametric rate in Proposition Proposition: Sinkhorn Divergences Interpolate the Rates holds for fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ. If the goal is to approximate the unregularized OT value, one must also account for the regularization bias. In a typical bounded-cost finite-dimensional regime,

∣Lˉcϵ(α,β)−Lc(α,β)∣≤Cϵ,E∣Lˉcϵ(α^n,β^n)−Lˉcϵ(α,β)∣≤Cc,dϵ−d/2n−1/2.\abs{\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)-\MK_\c(\alpha,\beta)} \leq C\epsilon, \qquad \EE\abs{\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_n)-\bar\MK_\c^\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)} \leq C_{c,d}\epsilon^{-d/2}n^{-1/2}.∣∣​Lˉcϵ​(α,β)−Lc​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cϵ,E∣∣​Lˉcϵ​(α^n​,β^​n​)−Lˉcϵ​(α,β)∣∣​≤Cc,d​ϵ−d/2n−1/2.
(17)

Balancing the two terms gives ϵ≃n−1/(d+2)\epsilon\simeq n^{-1/(d+2)}ϵ≃n−1/(d+2) and total error of order n−1/(d+2)n^{-1/(d+2)}n−1/(d+2). Equivalently, target accuracy η\etaη requires choosing ϵ≃η\epsilon\simeq\etaϵ≃η and n≃η−(d+2)n\simeq\eta^{-(d+2)}n≃η−(d+2) samples under this bound. Thus entropic smoothing improves the statistical behavior at fixed scale, but approximating exact OT still forces a bias-variance tradeoff whose exponent deteriorates with dimension.

The interactive demo below is only a scaling guide: change the dimension to see the exact-OT exponent flatten, and change ϵ\epsilonϵ to move the Sinkhorn bias floor against its parametric fluctuation term.

Interactive panel. This exploratory panel is a scaling guide. Use dimension, sample size, and epsilon to compare statistical fluctuation with regularization bias.

Bias and Variance of OT¶

The previous section answered a coarse but essential question: how many samples are needed before an empirical OT quantity is accurate, typically up to universal constants. We now ask for a finer statistical description. Rather than only estimating the size of the error, we seek the first expansion of the plug-in estimator. Given empirical laws α^n\hat\alpha_nα^n​ and β^m\hat\beta_mβ^​m​, and an OT functional TTT, this expansion separates the leading statistical bias

Bn,m(T):=ET(α^n,β^m)−T(α,β)B_{n,m}(T) \eqdef \mathbb E T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-T(\alpha,\beta)Bn,m​(T):=ET(α^n​,β^​m​)−T(α,β)
(18)

and the centered fluctuation

Zn,m(T):=T(α^n,β^m)−ET(α^n,β^m),Z_{n,m}(T) \eqdef T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-\mathbb E T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m),Zn,m​(T):=T(α^n​,β^​m​)−ET(α^n​,β^​m​),
(19)

for OT functionals such as T(α,β)=Wpp(α,β)T(\alpha,\beta)=\Wass_p^p(\alpha,\beta)T(α,β)=Wpp​(α,β), T(α,β)=Lc(α,β)T(\alpha,\beta)=\mathcal L_c(\alpha,\beta)T(α,β)=Lc​(α,β), or their entropic analogues. This is not just the central limit theorem of Law of Large Numbers and Central Limit Theorem, which describes the random signed measure n(α^n−α)\sqrt n(\hat\alpha_n-\alpha)n​(α^n​−α) itself. Here the empirical process is pushed through a nonlinear, often nonsmooth, transport value. The resulting law is governed by the local differentiability of the OT functional and by the geometry of its optimal dual potentials.

Literature Map¶

The shape of the limit depends sharply on the analytic regularity of the transport value. For exact OT on a finite or countable space, the map from empirical weights to optimal cost is convex and piecewise affine. Sommerfeld and Munk Sommerfeld & Munk, 2018, and the countable-space extensions of Tameling--Sommerfeld--Munk and Hundrieser--Klatt--Munk--Tameling Tameling et al., 2017Hundrieser et al., 2022, show that the natural limit is a directional delta-method limit: often a support function of a Gaussian process over an optimal dual face, rather than a Gaussian random variable. For Euclidean costs, del Barrio--Loubes and collaborators obtain central-limit theorems for empirical transportation costs under regularity and uniqueness assumptions on the Kantorovich potentials Barrio & Loubes, 2017Barrio et al., 2021. Entropic regularization makes the problem smoother. For fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, the value is differentiable with respect to the marginals, and the limiting variance is read directly from the entropic dual potentials. This is the point of view developed by Bigot--Cazelles--Papadakis, Klatt--Tameling--Munk, Hundrieser--Klatt--Munk, and Mena--Weed Bigot et al., 2019Klatt et al., 2020Hundrieser et al., 2021Mena & Weed, 2019. More recent results clarify weak limits beyond smooth costs and second-order null limits for Sinkhorn divergences González-Sanz & Hundrieser, 2023Goldfeld et al., 2022. These asymptotic statements do not replace the dimension-dependent bounds of Sample Complexity; they explain what the leading random object is, what constants appear, and where the bias comes from.

Bias Versus Centered Fluctuation¶

The decomposition

T(α^n,β^m)−T(α,β)=ET(α^n,β^m)−T(α,β)⏟bias+T(α^n,β^m)−ET(α^n,β^m)⏟centered fluctuationT(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-T(\alpha,\beta) = \underbrace{\mathbb E T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-T(\alpha,\beta)}_{\text{bias}} + \underbrace{T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-\mathbb E T(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)}_{\text{centered fluctuation}}T(α^n​,β^​m​)−T(α,β)=biasET(α^n​,β^​m​)−T(α,β)​​+centered fluctuationT(α^n​,β^​m​)−ET(α^n​,β^​m​)​​
(20)

is elementary, but in OT it is genuinely diagnostic because the two terms may live on different scales. For the self-distance T(α^n,α)=Wpp(α^n,α)T(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)=\Wass_p^p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)T(α^n​,α)=Wpp​(α^n​,α), the population value is zero and the deterministic bias EWpp(α^n,α)\mathbb E\Wass_p^p(\hat\alpha_n,\alpha)EWpp​(α^n​,α) is the leading term. On a regular d′d'd′-dimensional support, the high-dimensional matching scale is n−p/d′n^{-p/d'}n−p/d′ when d′>2pd'>2pd′>2p, with a critical logarithmic correction when d′=2pd'=2pd′=2p. Below this threshold the rate is controlled by empirical-process fluctuations and can be faster; in one dimension, smooth positive densities give the familiar quantile-process asymptotics for Wpp\Wass_p^pWpp​, of order n−p/2n^{-p/2}n−p/2 for p>1p>1p>1, and in particular n−1n^{-1}n−1 for W22\Wass_2^2W22​. Thus p/d′p/d'p/d′ is the high-dimensional matching exponent, not a universal rate formula Dudley, 1969Fournier & Guillin, 2015Weed & Bach, 2019. By contrast, when T(α,β)T(\alpha,\beta)T(α,β) is nonzero and the OT functional is differentiable at (α,β)(\alpha,\beta)(α,β), the centered fluctuation is often governed by an ordinary n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 central limit theorem. A ``slow OT statistic’’ can therefore have three distinct causes: a large empirical bias, a nonsmooth directional limit, or a regularization bias Tϵ(α,β)−T0(α,β)T_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)-T_0(\alpha,\beta)Tϵ​(α,β)−T0​(α,β) that still has to be removed.

Proposition: Finite-Space Bias and CLT for Exact OT

Let a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​ and b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​ have positive entries, let C∈Rn×m\C\in\RR^{n\times m}C∈Rn×m, and define

Φ(a,b):=LC(a,b)=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨C,P⟩.\Phi(a,b)\eqdef \mathcal L_\C(a,b) = \min_{\P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)}\dotp{\C}{\P}.Φ(a,b):=LC​(a,b)=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨C,P⟩.
(21)

Let a^N\hat a_Na^N​ be the empirical histogram of NNN independent samples from aaa, while bbb is fixed. Denote by

D⋆(a,b):=arg max⁡fi+gj≤Cij⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩\mathcal D^\star(a,b) \eqdef \argmax_{f_i+g_j\leq \C_{ij}} \dotp{f}{a}+\dotp{g}{b}D⋆(a,b):=fi​+gj​≤Cij​argmax​⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩
(22)

the set of optimal dual vectors, modulo the gauge (f,g)↦(f+λ1,g−λ1)(f,g)\mapsto(f+\lambda\ones,g-\lambda\ones)(f,g)↦(f+λ1,g−λ1). If GaG_aGa​ is a centered Gaussian vector with covariance

E GaGa⊤=diag⁡(a)−aa⊤,\mathbb E\,G_aG_a^\top=\operatorname{diag}(a)-aa^\top,EGa​Ga⊤​=diag(a)−aa⊤,
(23)

then

N(Φ(a^N,b)−Φ(a,b))⟹sup⁡(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)⟨f,Ga⟩.\sqrt N\big(\Phi(\hat a_N,b)-\Phi(a,b)\big) \Longrightarrow \sup_{(f,g)\in\mathcal D^\star(a,b)}\dotp{f}{G_a}.N​(Φ(a^N​,b)−Φ(a,b))⟹(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)sup​⟨f,Ga​⟩.
(24)

Moreover,

N(EΦ(a^N,b)−Φ(a,b))⟶Esup⁡(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)⟨f,Ga⟩,\sqrt N\big(\mathbb E\Phi(\hat a_N,b)-\Phi(a,b)\big) \longrightarrow \mathbb E\sup_{(f,g)\in\mathcal D^\star(a,b)}\dotp{f}{G_a},N​(EΦ(a^N​,b)−Φ(a,b))⟶E(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)sup​⟨f,Ga​⟩,
(25)

and the rescaled variance converges to the variance of the same limit. If the source dual potential f⋆f^\starf⋆ is unique up to constants, the limit is Gaussian with variance

σOT2=∑iai(fi⋆−∑kakfk⋆)2.\sigma_{\mathrm{OT}}^2 = \sum_i a_i \left(f^\star_i-\sum_k a_k f^\star_k\right)^2.σOT2​=i∑​ai​(fi⋆​−k∑​ak​fk⋆​)2.
(26)

In this differentiable case, the first-order bias vanishes:

EΦ(a^N,b)−Φ(a,b)=o(N−1/2).\mathbb E\Phi(\hat a_N,b)-\Phi(a,b)=o(N^{-1/2}).EΦ(a^N​,b)−Φ(a,b)=o(N−1/2).
(27)
Proof

The multinomial central limit theorem gives

N(a^N−a)⟹Ga,Ga∈{ξ:⟨1,ξ⟩=0}.\sqrt N(\hat a_N-a)\Longrightarrow G_a, \qquad G_a\in\{\xi:\dotp{\ones}{\xi}=0\}.N​(a^N​−a)⟹Ga​,Ga​∈{ξ:⟨1,ξ⟩=0}.
(28)

The dual formulation writes Φ\PhiΦ as the supremum, over the feasible polyhedron fi+gj≤Cijf_i+g_j\leq\C_{ij}fi​+gj​≤Cij​, of the affine functions a↦⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩a\mapsto\dotp f a+\dotp g ba↦⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩. Its directional derivative at aaa, in any tangent direction hhh with ⟨1,h⟩=0\dotp{\ones}{h}=0⟨1,h⟩=0, is therefore

DaΦ(a,b)[h]=sup⁡(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)⟨f,h⟩,D_a\Phi(a,b)[h] = \sup_{(f,g)\in\mathcal D^\star(a,b)}\dotp f h,Da​Φ(a,b)[h]=(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)sup​⟨f,h⟩,
(29)

which is the finite-dimensional form of Danskin’s theorem already used in Proposition: First variations of unregularized OT. The directional delta method gives the displayed distributional limit. After fixing a gauge, the relevant dual face is bounded, so the support functions above have at most linear growth in the multinomial fluctuation. Uniform integrability then yields convergence of the first two moments, hence the bias and variance statements. If f⋆f^\starf⋆ is unique modulo constants, the support function reduces to the linear form ⟨f⋆,Ga⟩\dotp{f^\star}{G_a}⟨f⋆,Ga​⟩. Its expectation is zero, and the covariance of GaG_aGa​ gives exactly the displayed variance.

The same argument gives the independent two-sample limit. If N/(N+M)→λ∈(0,1)N/(N+M)\to\lambda\in(0,1)N/(N+M)→λ∈(0,1), then, with rN,M=NM/(N+M)r_{N,M}=\sqrt{NM/(N+M)}rN,M​=NM/(N+M)​,

rN,M(Φ(a^N,b^M)−Φ(a,b))⟹sup⁡(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)(1−λ ⟨f,Ga⟩+λ ⟨g,Gb⟩),r_{N,M}\big(\Phi(\hat a_N,\hat b_M)-\Phi(a,b)\big) \Longrightarrow \sup_{(f,g)\in\mathcal D^\star(a,b)} \left( \sqrt{1-\lambda}\,\dotp{f}{G_a} + \sqrt{\lambda}\,\dotp{g}{G_b} \right),rN,M​(Φ(a^N​,b^M​)−Φ(a,b))⟹(f,g)∈D⋆(a,b)sup​(1−λ​⟨f,Ga​⟩+λ​⟨g,Gb​⟩),
(30)

where GaG_aGa​ and GbG_bGb​ are independent multinomial Gaussian limits. When both dual potentials are unique, the first-order bias is negligible and the asymptotic variance of the unscaled estimator is

1N∑iai(fi⋆−fˉ⋆)2+1M∑jbj(gj⋆−gˉ⋆)2,fˉ⋆=∑iaifi⋆,gˉ⋆=∑jbjgj⋆.\frac{1}{N}\sum_i a_i(f^\star_i-\bar f^\star)^2 + \frac{1}{M}\sum_j b_j(g^\star_j-\bar g^\star)^2, \qquad \bar f^\star=\sum_i a_i f_i^\star, \quad \bar g^\star=\sum_j b_j g_j^\star.N1​i∑​ai​(fi⋆​−fˉ​⋆)2+M1​j∑​bj​(gj⋆​−gˉ​⋆)2,fˉ​⋆=i∑​ai​fi⋆​,gˉ​⋆=j∑​bj​gj⋆​.
(31)

If the optimal face contains several dual potentials, the limit is the supremum of a Gaussian process over that face and need not itself be Gaussian. The leading bias is then the expectation of this supremum divided by the square-root sample size. This nonsmoothness is the basic reason why inference for exact OT can remain delicate even on a finite space.

Proposition: Finite-Space Bias and CLT for Entropic OT

Fix ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, positive histograms a,ba,ba,b, and a finite cost matrix C\CC. Consider the KL-normalized entropic value

Φϵ(a,b):=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨C,P⟩+ϵKL⁡(P∣a⊗b).\Phi_\epsilon(a,b) \eqdef \min_{\P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \dotp{\C}{\P}+\epsilon\operatorname{KL}(\P|a\otimes b).Φϵ​(a,b):=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨C,P⟩+ϵKL(P∣a⊗b).
(32)

Let (fϵ,gϵ)(f_\epsilon,g_\epsilon)(fϵ​,gϵ​) be normalized entropic dual potentials for Φϵ(a,b)\Phi_\epsilon(a,b)Φϵ​(a,b). If a^N\hat a_Na^N​ is the empirical histogram of NNN samples from aaa, then

N(Φϵ(a^N,b)−Φϵ(a,b))⟹N(0,σϵ,a2),\sqrt N\big(\Phi_\epsilon(\hat a_N,b)-\Phi_\epsilon(a,b)\big) \Longrightarrow \mathcal N(0,\sigma_{\epsilon,a}^2),N​(Φϵ​(a^N​,b)−Φϵ​(a,b))⟹N(0,σϵ,a2​),
(33)

and

EΦϵ(a^N,b)−Φϵ(a,b)=O(N−1),N Var⁡(Φϵ(a^N,b))→σϵ,a2,\mathbb E\Phi_\epsilon(\hat a_N,b)-\Phi_\epsilon(a,b)=O(N^{-1}), \qquad N\,\operatorname{Var}\big(\Phi_\epsilon(\hat a_N,b)\big)\to\sigma_{\epsilon,a}^2,EΦϵ​(a^N​,b)−Φϵ​(a,b)=O(N−1),NVar(Φϵ​(a^N​,b))→σϵ,a2​,
(34)

where

σϵ,a2=∑iai((fϵ)i−∑kak(fϵ)k)2.\sigma_{\epsilon,a}^2 = \sum_i a_i \left((f_\epsilon)_i-\sum_k a_k(f_\epsilon)_k\right)^2.σϵ,a2​=i∑​ai​((fϵ​)i​−k∑​ak​(fϵ​)k​)2.
(35)

For two independent empirical histograms, the first-order asymptotic variance is

σϵ,a2N+σϵ,b2M,σϵ,b2=∑jbj((gϵ)j−∑ℓbℓ(gϵ)ℓ)2.\frac{\sigma_{\epsilon,a}^2}{N} + \frac{\sigma_{\epsilon,b}^2}{M}, \qquad \sigma_{\epsilon,b}^2 = \sum_j b_j \left((g_\epsilon)_j-\sum_\ell b_\ell(g_\epsilon)_\ell\right)^2.Nσϵ,a2​​+Mσϵ,b2​​,σϵ,b2​=j∑​bj​((gϵ​)j​−ℓ∑​bℓ​(gϵ​)ℓ​)2.
(36)
Proof

For positive histograms and fixed ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, the entropic problem has a unique optimizer, and the KL-normalized value is smooth on a neighborhood of (a,b)(a,b)(a,b) inside the relative interiors of the simplices. Proposition: First variations of entropic OT gives

DaΦϵ(a,b)[h]=⟨fϵ,h⟩.D_a\Phi_\epsilon(a,b)[h]=\dotp{f_\epsilon}{h}.Da​Φϵ​(a,b)[h]=⟨fϵ​,h⟩.
(37)

Applying the ordinary delta method to the multinomial CLT N(a^N−a)⇒Ga\sqrt N(\hat a_N-a)\Rightarrow G_aN​(a^N​−a)⇒Ga​ gives the one-sample limit ⟨fϵ,Ga⟩\dotp{f_\epsilon}{G_a}⟨fϵ​,Ga​⟩, whose variance is the displayed covariance formula. The only boundary nuisance is that an empirical histogram may have a zero entry. Since ai>0a_i>0ai​>0 for all iii, this event has probability at most ∑i(1−ai)N\sum_i(1-a_i)^N∑i​(1−ai​)N, hence is exponentially small and does not affect the first-order limit or the N−1N^{-1}N−1 bias. On the complementary event, Taylor’s formula applies in the relative interior: the first-order term is centered and the Hessian is locally bounded, so the plug-in bias is O(N−1)O(N^{-1})O(N−1). The variance convergence follows from the same expansion and from bounded moments of the multinomial fluctuation. The two-sample formula follows by applying the argument independently to the two marginals.

For the entropy-only convention LCϵ\mathcal L_\C^\epsilonLCϵ​ used in Entropic Regularization for Discrete Measures, the marginal entropy derivatives described after Proposition: First variations of entropic OT must be added to the potentials. For the debiased Sinkhorn divergence Lˉcϵ\bar\MK_c^\epsilonLˉcϵ​, the derivative is instead the difference between cross and self entropic potentials. At the null α=β\alpha=\betaα=β, this first derivative can vanish for the debiased statistic, and second-order limits then become relevant Goldfeld et al., 2022. This is a useful warning: the asymptotic law is determined not only by a rate, but also by the local geometry of the functional.

Three Error Terms When Entropy Estimates Exact OT¶

If the target is the exact cost T0(α,β)=Lc(α,β)T_0(\alpha,\beta)=\mathcal L_c(\alpha,\beta)T0​(α,β)=Lc​(α,β) but the statistic uses TϵT_\epsilonTϵ​, three errors should be kept separate:

Tϵ(α^n,β^m)−T0(α,β)=Tϵ(α,β)−T0(α,β)⏟regularization bias+ETϵ(α^n,β^m)−Tϵ(α,β)⏟statistical bias+Zn,m(Tϵ)⏟centered fluctuation.T_\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-T_0(\alpha,\beta) = \underbrace{T_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)-T_0(\alpha,\beta)}_{\text{regularization bias}} + \underbrace{\mathbb E T_\epsilon(\hat\alpha_n,\hat\beta_m)-T_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)}_{\text{statistical bias}} + \underbrace{Z_{n,m}(T_\epsilon)}_{\text{centered fluctuation}} .Tϵ​(α^n​,β^​m​)−T0​(α,β)=regularization biasTϵ​(α,β)−T0​(α,β)​​+statistical biasETϵ​(α^n​,β^​m​)−Tϵ​(α,β)​​+centered fluctuationZn,m​(Tϵ​)​​.
(38)

This identity is trivial algebraically but important statistically. It is usually read after choosing ϵ=ϵn\epsilon=\epsilon_nϵ=ϵn​. For fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ, the statistical bias is typically O(n−1+m−1)O(n^{-1}+m^{-1})O(n−1+m−1) in finite dimension, and the centered fluctuation is OP(n−1/2+m−1/2)O_{\mathbb P}(n^{-1/2}+m^{-1/2})OP​(n−1/2+m−1/2); boundary events where an empirical finite histogram has a zero entry have exponentially small probability when the population weights are positive. The regularization bias disappears only when ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0, whereas the variance constants usually deteriorate as ϵ↓0\epsilon\downarrow0ϵ↓0. This is the asymptotic form of the bias--variance tradeoff illustrated in Div.

What Changes in Continuous Spaces¶

The finite-dimensional formulas above are not merely toy models; they are the cleanest template for the general mechanism. Whenever an OT value is Hadamard differentiable and admits a unique sufficiently regular dual potential f⋆f^\starf⋆, the empirical-process CLT and the delta method give a first-order Gaussian limit with one-sample variance Var⁡α(f⋆(X))\operatorname{Var}_\alpha(f^\star(X))Varα​(f⋆(X)). When differentiability fails, as can happen for exact OT because the dual optimizer is not unique or because one studies the degenerate self-distance T(α,α)=0T(\alpha,\alpha)=0T(α,α)=0, the limit may be non-Gaussian or may occur at a slower non-parametric scale. Entropic regularization smooths the dual potentials and typically restores a conventional first-order CLT for fixed ϵ\epsilonϵ, even under weak assumptions on the cost. If the target is unregularized OT, however, this statistical expansion must still be balanced against the bias Tϵ(α,β)−T0(α,β)T_\epsilon(\alpha,\beta)-T_0(\alpha,\beta)Tϵ​(α,β)−T0​(α,β). Thus Sample Complexity gives dimension-dependent magnitudes, while the present section identifies the local asymptotic constants and covariance formulas needed for inference.

References¶
  1. Berry, A. C. (1941). The Accuracy of the Gaussian Approximation to the Sum of Independent Variates. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society, 49(1), 122–136.
  2. Esseen, C.-G. (1942). On the Liapunoff Limit of Error in the Theory of Probability. Arkiv for Matematik, Astronomi Och Fysik, 28A(9), 1–19.
  3. Chen, L. H. Y., Goldstein, L., & Shao, Q.-M. (2011). Normal Approximation by Stein’s Method. Springer.
  4. Bobkov, S. G. (2018). Berry–Esseen bounds and Edgeworth expansions in the central limit theorem for transport distances. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 170, 229–262.
  5. Rio, E. (2011). Asymptotic constants for minimal distance in the central limit theorem. Electronic Communications in Probability, 16, 96–103.
  6. Dudley, R. M. (1969). The speed of mean Glivenko-Cantelli convergence. Annals of Mathematical Statistics, 40(1), 40–50.
  7. Weed, J., & Bach, F. (2019). Sharp asymptotic and finite-sample rates of convergence of empirical measures in Wasserstein distance. Bernoulli, 25(4A), 2620–2648. 10.3150/18-BEJ1065
  8. Genevay, A., Chizat, L., Bach, F., Cuturi, M., & Peyré, G. (2019). Sample Complexity of Sinkhorn Divergences. Proceedings of the Twenty-Second International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, 89, 1574–1583.
  9. Bigot, J., Cazelles, E., & Papadakis, N. (2019). Central limit theorems for entropy-regularized optimal transport on finite spaces and statistical applications. Electronic Journal of Statistics, 13(2), 5120–5150. 10.1214/19-EJS1637
  10. Ramdas, A., Trillos, N. G., & Cuturi, M. (2017). On Wasserstein two-sample testing and related families of nonparametric tests. Entropy, 19(2), 47.
  11. Gretton, A., Borgwardt, K. M., Rasch, M. J., Schölkopf, B., & Smola, A. (2012). A kernel two-sample test. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 13(Mar), 723–773.
  12. Fournier, N., & Guillin, A. (2015). On the rate of convergence in Wasserstein distance of the empirical measure. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 162(3–4), 707–738.
  13. Sommerfeld, M., & Munk, A. (2018). Inference for empirical Wasserstein distances on finite spaces. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Statistical Methodology), 80(1), 219–238.
  14. Tameling, C., Sommerfeld, M., & Munk, A. (2017). Empirical optimal transport on countable metric spaces: Distributional limits and statistical applications. arXiv Preprint arXiv:1707.00973.
  15. Hundrieser, S., Klatt, M., Munk, A., & Tameling, C. (2022). Empirical optimal transport on countable metric spaces: Distributional limits and statistical applications. Annals of Applied Probability, 32(5), 3869–3902.
